front: Figure: second-largest ethnic group in New Zealand, after the Pakeha (European New Zealanders)
Figure: traditionally most concentrated in the districts of Opotiki and Wairoa
Figure: legendarily migrated from Hawaiki aboard seven named great canoes (waka), including Tainui, Mataatua, and Nukutere
Figure: granted a 99-year lease on a geyser hotspot near the destroyed Pink and White Terraces to incentivize cultural tourism
Figure: burned the tree gum kapia as an insecticide, sparking a lucrative kauri-gum mining craze in the mid-1800s
Figure: contact with Europeans declined after some of these people attacked the docked missionary ship Boyd
Figure: a member of this ethnicity complained about the book This Horrid Practice, which discussed cannibalism among them
Event: named for the flintlock firearms first obtained at Bay of Islands whaling stations in exchange for pigs, potatoes, and preserved tattooed heads (mokomokai)
Event: Ngapuhi chief Hongi Hika, who personally received a suit of armor from King George IV while touring England, is credited with sparking this conflict
Event: fighting was accelerated by cultivation of the potato variety taewa, which freed more men for war
Event: displaced the Ngati Mutunga and Ngati Tama iwi, who fled to the Chatham Islands and massacred the Moriori people there in 1835
Event: Angela Ballara's history of these events denies the "fatal impact" theory and is critiqued in Cannibal Talk by Gananath Obeyesekere
Event: in the "Elizabeth affair," James Stewart ferried warriors seeking utu (revenge) in exchange for a cargo of flax
Event: the Battle of Hingakaka, precipitated by a dispute over kahawai fish, saw the sacred relic Te Uenuku lost for about 100 years
Event: a missionary's journal, written while accompanying the Ngapuhi tribe on a war expedition, gives a firsthand account of these conflicts
Treaty: hastily drafted by Governor William Hobson, this 1840 document had difficulty translating the English word "sovereignty"
Treaty: its Maori-language text is often called simply Te Tiriti
Treaty: signed by over 500 Maori chiefs (rangatira), who ceded "sovereignty" while the Crown confirmed their territorial rights
Treaty: an 1877 court case declared this document a "simple nullity"
Treaty: the Maori phrase for "absolute sovereignty" — which this treaty did not actually cede to Britain — is now represented by a black, white, and red flag
Treaty: a 1975 act established the Waitangi Tribunal to investigate breaches of this document
Treaty: an earlier 1839 agreement had granted the Maori tribal autonomy from the British Crown
Treaty: in its wake, the British Crown fought a series of wars against Maori tribes from 1845 to 1872 over rising settler numbers
Event: in 1835, around 900 Maori of the Ngati Mutunga and Ngati Tama iwi, displaced during the Musket Wars, hijacked a ship and killed or enslaved most of the pacifist Moriori
Event: the Moriori had followed the nonviolent Law of Nunuku, a 16th-century leader
Event: dendroglyphs called rakau, made by an offshoot Maori population on the Chatham Islands, are protected by the Hapupu Reserve
Event: Percy Smith and Elsdon Best promoted a racist theory that the Moriori were the last remnant of a paleolithic culture wiped out by a superior "Great Fleet" race
Event: Alexander Shand's conversations with Hirawanu Tapu are the major source for knowledge of the Moriori language
Event: the Moriori are inaccurately depicted laboring on American-style plantations in the first chapter of David Mitchell's Cloud Atlas
Event: in 1994, a tribunal hearing named for the town of Waitangi formally recognized the Moriori as the Chatham Islands' original inhabitants
Event: historian T. C. Winegard compared this genocide to Tasmania's Black War
War: Ngapuhi chief Hone Heke repeatedly cut down the British flagstaff at Kororareka — which he had earlier gifted — starting this 1845 war
War: in response to the flagstaff's felling, the Royal Navy ship HMS Hazard bombarded the town of Kororareka
War: also called the Northern War or the First Maori War
War: Heke's forces lost the Battle of Ruapekapeka partly because many soldiers, having converted to Christianity, were absent observing Sunday church
Institution: founded in the 1850s to create a role equivalent to a European monarch and unify tribes against land sales
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: its cultural and political center was at Waikato, with Potatau I as first king
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: Governor George Grey targeted this movement's holder in the "illegal" Waikato Invasion
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: General Duncan Cameron crushed a related revolt in the Taranaki region, with soldiers stampeding from a fort after being surprised by defenders emerging from underground bunkers
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: warlord Rewi declared "we will fight on forever and ever and ever" at a losing battle, after which followers turned to peaceful resistance
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: in 1995, Elizabeth II personally apologized to this movement's holder (a successor of Tawhiao) for 1860s land confiscations that had targeted it
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: land confiscation, known as "raupatu," was undertaken as punishment against supporters of this movement
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: during World War I, Te Puea Herangi led this movement in opposing British conscription of Maori men, arguing they should not fight for a Crown responsible for the Treaty of Waitangi
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: a Maori "Pioneer Battalion" nonetheless fought at Gallipoli alongside forces commanded by William Birdwood
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: this movement's holders warily granted sanctuary to the founder of the Ringatu religion (Te Kooti)
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: the founder of the "Hauhau" religion (Pai Marire) was a supporter of this movement
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: a parliament house, the Hikurangi, was founded by Maori at Papawai as part of a related self-governance movement
work: King Movement (Kingitanga) & the New Zealand Wars

front: Institution: Pai Marire ("Hauhau") founder Te Ua Haumene taught that shouting "Hapa" granted protection from bullets, a belief disproved at the Sentry Hill massacre
work: Prophetic Movements: Pai Marire, Ringatu & Ratana

front: Institution: Te Ua Haumene advocated erecting niu poles after receiving a vision of the angel Gabriel
work: Prophetic Movements: Pai Marire, Ringatu & Ratana

front: Institution: a Pai Marire follower was blamed for the hanging of missionary Carl Volkner
work: Prophetic Movements: Pai Marire, Ringatu & Ratana

front: Institution: Te Kooti founded this church after leading a guerrilla campaign that began with a raid on Poverty Bay
work: Prophetic Movements: Pai Marire, Ringatu & Ratana

front: Institution: this church calls for the rejection of spiritualists and healers known as tohunga
work: Prophetic Movements: Pai Marire, Ringatu & Ratana

front: Institution: Whina Cooper led a 29-day 1975 Land March protesting Maori land theft by pakeha
Institution: an activist group whose name translates to "the Young Warriors" (Nga Tamatoa) confronted engineering students preparing a 1979 capping stunt
Institution: activists later occupied Raglan Golf Course and Bastion Point
Institution: the government set a controversial billion-dollar "fiscal envelope" limiting the total value of Maori land claims
Institution: under Eddie Durie, the Waitangi Tribunal sided with Maori fishing rights against a synthetic-fuels plant promoted by the "Think Big" economic strategy
Institution: a political party was formed in 2004 to defend Maori claims to New Zealand's foreshore and seabed
Institution: the foreshore and seabed are also subject to claims protected by seats specifically allocated to Maori in the national legislature
Institution: four special Maori electorates, created in 1867, were indefinitely extended in 1876 and increased in 1996 following the Royal Commission on the Electoral System
Institution: that 1996 increase followed New Zealand's adoption of MMP (Mixed Member Proportional representation) after 1992-93 referenda
Institution: a national referendum rejected a proposed New Zealand flag design featuring the koru (unfurling silver fern) symbol
Institution: under Prime Minister Helen Clark, affirmative-action policies benefiting Maori were opposed by the National Party
Institution: psychologist David Ausubel's Fulbright-funded research studied discrimination against Maori in the 1950s
Institution: Frederick Maning chronicled Maori history, and James Carroll became the first Maori to serve as acting head of government (Prime Minister) of New Zealand
Institution: Maori gained universal suffrage in 1893; in conjunction with the WCTU, they organized Nga Komiti Wahine to address suffrage and prohibition
Institution: the Ngai Tahu iwi reclaimed a South Island land settlement, which they call Te Waipounamu, in 1997
Institution: since some dialects merge "ng" with "k," Kai Tahu and Ngai Tahu are both terms for the same iwi
Language: uses a 15-letter alphabet including the digraphs "ng" and "wh"
Language: the greeting kia ora comes from this language
Language: after most speakers shifted to English in the 20th century, revitalization pioneered the immersion-based "language nest" (kohanga reo) model
Language: features grammatical clusivity: distinct pronouns (matou vs. tatou) for "we" depending on whether the addressee is included
Language: belongs to the Austronesian family and displays ergative-type alignment
Language: politician Winston Peters opposed a proposal to adopt this language's word Aotearoa as New Zealand's official name
Language: a hill in the Hawke's Bay region has an 85-letter place name in this language, the world's longest single-word place name
Language: place names include the Kilmog (from a phrase for a broom tea-tree species) and the fjord Piopiotahi (named for the piopio bird)
Language: became an official language of New Zealand as part of a widespread 20th-century cultural revival
Culture: traditionally cut into the skin using uhi (chisels), leaving permanent grooves unlike Western tattooing's rotary or coil guns
Culture: uses a pigment called ngarahu, made from bird or fish oil mixed with soot from burned kauri wood
Culture: one of the earliest styles is the kuri pattern, a lattice of three horizontal and vertical lines
Culture: one origin story holds that the underworld spirit Niwareka's father taught the art to her husband Mataora after sweat caused his pigment to run down his face
Culture: men traditionally wear it across the entire face, while the traditional style for women covers only the lips and chin
Culture: anthropologist Mead connected its facial patterns to Lapita motifs, alongside the jade-carving tradition of pounamu
Culture: welcome ceremonies (powhiri) at marae meeting grounds begin with a karanga summons and are sometimes followed by haka war dances
Culture: meetinghouses (wharenui) are decorated with the unfurling koru fern-frond motif
Culture: the All Blacks rugby team performs the haka, a rhythmic shout with feet-stamping, before games
Culture: the haka was traditionally performed to herald the arrival of the goddess of summer
Culture: poi (two balls connected by string, twirled over musical accompaniment) is a performing art of this people
Culture: geographer Evelyn Stokes demonstrated the "long" style of poi swinging on American television
Culture: after three days of mourning at a marae, the recently deceased are buried in tangi funeral rites
Culture: Raymond Firth classified four levels of genealogy (whakapapa), recited to enforce cultural identity and lend authority to personal mana
Culture: jade and similar minerals, called "greenstone" (pounamu), are carved into ceremonial weapons like mere
Culture: strong peace treaties were traditionally referred to as "greenstone"
Culture: the microtonal chanting style moteatea was collected by politician Apirana Ngata
Culture: oriori lullabies and farewell laments may be choreographed with hand trembling and showing the whites of one's eyes
Culture: the taonga puoro instrument tradition includes a conch-shell trumpet called putatara
Culture: social units called iwi (tribes), made up of hapu, manage national parks like Tongariro, Fiordland, and Aoraki/Mount Cook
Culture: in pre-contact warfare, the first person killed (the "first fish") had his heart cut out and offered to the gods
Culture: in one campaign, 100 warriors were smuggled ashore wrapped in kuri-dog skins disguised as a beached whale
Culture: gardens often containing Cordyline and made with ko sticks traditionally centered on the kumara variety of sweet potato
Culture: an earthwork pa fort was constructed on One Tree Hill, overlooking a city they call Tamaki Makaurau (Auckland)
Culture: built terraced hill forts called pa, whose language was documented by missionary Thomas Kendall
Culture: in 1983, songwriters Ngoi Pewhairangi and Dalvanius Prime wrote "Poi E," the first Maori-language pop song, which outsold international artists in New Zealand
Culture: the sky-father Rangi and earth-mother Papa were locked in such a tight embrace that their children suffocated, until the forest god Tane forced them apart to let in light
Culture: the trickster hero Maui, son of the sea god Tangaroa and guardian of fish, used a magic fishhook to pull up islands, including the North Island, from the sea
Culture: Maui tricked the fire goddess Mahuika into giving him fire by taking her fingernails one by one
Culture: Maui died attempting to win immortality by passing through the underworld goddess Hine-nui-te-po, ruler of the underworld
Culture: the first woman was created from a pool of water that cast a reflection of the first man, Tiki
Culture: deities are often depicted on carved "god sticks"; myths were first written down by George Grey while governor of New Zealand
Culture: tribal groups are protected by fish- or reptile-like guardian spirits called taniwha
Culture: after his in-laws tried to murder him, Tawhaki was nursed back to health with fire fueled by a single giant log
Culture: Tawhaki then climbed to heaven on a giant vine with the aid of his grandmother, a cannibalistic thunder goddess whose sight he restored
Culture: the explorer-ancestor Kupe discovered the "land of the long white cloud" (Aotearoa) after eloping in an ocean-faring canoe and killing the monster Wheke
Culture: the child-snatching monster Te Hokioi was likely inspired by encounters with the extinct Haast's Eagle
Culture: Tinirau, son of Tangaroa and guardian of fish, lent his pet whale to Kae for a return journey, only for Kae to kill and eat it
Culture: the story of Tutira and Kawariki features the sea god Tangaroa and the primordial pair Rangi and Papa
Culture: an agricultural god used a secret stash of string to win the world's first kite-flying contest
Culture: the cave Taiwhetuki is the entrance to the underworld
Culture: the Milky Way is represented by the great fish Te Ikaroa, carried to the heavens by Kewa, a son of Papa and Rangi
Novel: Alan Duff's 1994 novel follows Jake Heke's alcoholism and domestic violence against his wife Beth
Film: adapted into a 1994 film directed by Lee Tamahori
Novel: Beth's eldest son joins the Brown Fists gang, and she becomes homeless after discovering Grace hanged herself, before starting a culture group
Novel: Keri Hulme's 1984 novel, rejected by every major publisher until picked up by the feminist collective Spiral, won the Booker Prize
Novel: follows the mute boy Simon, adopted by Joe and Hana Gillayley, who befriends the artist Kerewin Holmes
Novel: is based on the Maori story of Paikea, who was guided by a whale to the South Island
Novel: centers on a succession crisis in a Maori family
Short Story: Katherine Mansfield's "How Pearl Button Was Killed" and "The Garden Party" (in which Laura Sheridan's family hosts the title event) feature Maori characters
Author: 2008 Neustadt Prize winner Patricia Grace was the first prominent female Maori writer
Novel: in Ngaio Marsh's Colour Scheme, Maurice Questing's death in a mud pool is connected to a Maori legend
Figure: opera soprano Kiri Te Kanawa, partially descended from the Maori, gave a controversial 2003 interview about them
